title: Hereditary Angioedema
targetAudience: Medical Students, Residents, Allergy/Immunology Fellows, Emergency Medicine
objectives: Classify hereditary angioedema types (HAE-1, HAE-2, HAE with normal C1-INH)
Interpret C4, C1-INH antigen, and C1-INH function for diagnosis
Recognize life-threatening laryngeal angioedema and initiate treatment
Apply on-demand, short-term prophylaxis, and long-term prophylaxis strategies
Differentiate hereditary from acquired C1-INH deficiency

guidelineReferences.name: WAO/EAACI HAE Guideline 2021
guidelineReferences.citation: Maurer M, et al. The international WAO/EAACI guideline for the management of hereditary angioedema - The 2021 revision and update. World Allergy Organ J. 2022;15(3):100627
keyPoints: All attacks should be treated early - consider on-demand for all attacks
Upper airway attacks MUST be treated immediately
C4 is best screening test; confirm with C1-INH antigen and function
Goal: complete control with zero attacks

guidelineReferences.name: US HAEA Medical Advisory Board Guidelines 2020
guidelineReferences.citation: Busse PJ, et al. US HAEA Medical Advisory Board 2020 Guidelines for the Management of Hereditary Angioedema. J Allergy Clin Immunol Pract. 2021;9(1):132-150

vignette.text:
A 24-year-old woman presents to the ED with progressive facial and lip swelling over the past 6 hours. She reports similar episodes occurring every 2-3 weeks since age 16, affecting her face, hands, feet, and abdomen. Abdominal attacks cause severe pain, nausea, and vomiting lasting 2-3 days. She notes her father has similar episodes and her paternal aunt died from 'throat swelling' at age 35.

She denies itching or hives with these episodes. She takes oral contraceptives and notes attacks worsened after starting them. Previous ED visits resulted in treatment with epinephrine and antihistamines with no improvement.

Past Medical History: Recurrent angioedema since age 16, no known allergies

Family History: Father with recurrent angioedema, paternal aunt died from laryngeal edema

Medications: Combined oral contraceptive

Allergies: None

physicalExam.HEENT: Significant lip swelling (asymmetric), mild tongue edema, no pharyngeal edema currently visible, NO urticaria
physicalExam.skin: No hives, no erythema, non-pitting edema of lips

questions.stem: This patient has recurrent angioedema WITHOUT urticaria, family history of similar episodes, and no response to antihistamines/epinephrine.
questions.question: How do you distinguish bradykinin-mediated from histamine-mediated angioedema?
questions.hint: Consider the presence of urticaria and response to standard allergy medications.

options.text: Bradykinin-mediated: NO urticaria, NO itching, does NOT respond to antihistamines/epinephrine/steroids, longer duration (2-5 days). Histamine-mediated: urticaria present, itching, responds to antihistamines/epinephrine
Urticaria is always present with angioedema
Family history is not relevant
All angioedema responds to antihistamines

questions.explanation: Key distinction: BRADYKININ-mediated (HAE, ACE-inhibitor angioedema): NO urticaria/hives, NO itching, does NOT respond to antihistamines, epinephrine, or corticosteroids, attacks last 2-5 days, can be hereditary. HISTAMINE-mediated (allergic, chronic urticaria): urticaria usually present, itching, responds to antihistamines/epinephrine, shorter duration. This patient has classic bradykinin features: no urticaria, no response to standard treatment, family history.

whyChain.answer: Epinephrine works by counteracting histamine effects and stabilizing mast cells. In bradykinin-mediated angioedema, mast cells are NOT involved - the edema is caused by bradykinin acting on vascular endothelium. Different pathway requires different treatment.

Hereditary angioedema is autosomal dominant - 75% have family history. Her father is affected and aunt died from laryngeal edema (HAE has 15-30% lifetime risk of fatal laryngeal attack if untreated). Family history + no urticaria + no antihistamine response = classic HAE presentation.

sources.text: StatPearls: Hereditary Angioedema. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: You are classifying types of hereditary angioedema.
questions.question: What are the types of hereditary angioedema and their laboratory findings?

options.text: HAE Type 1 (85%): Low C1-INH antigen AND function; HAE Type 2 (15%): Normal/high C1-INH antigen but LOW function; HAE with normal C1-INH: Normal C1-INH, mutations in FXII, plasminogen, others
C4 is normal in all HAE types
HAE always has elevated C1-INH
Only one type of HAE exists

questions.explanation: HAE Classification: (1) HAE Type 1 (~85%): SERPING1 mutations → decreased C1-INH SYNTHESIS → low C1-INH antigen AND low function; (2) HAE Type 2 (~15%): SERPING1 mutations → dysfunctional C1-INH → normal/elevated C1-INH antigen but LOW function; (3) HAE with normal C1-INH (HAE-nC1INH): Normal C1-INH quantity and function, caused by mutations in Factor XII, plasminogen, angiopoietin-1, kininogen-1, or unknown. C4 is LOW in Types 1 and 2.

whyChain.why: What type does this patient have?
whyChain.answer: She has HAE Type 1: Low C1-INH antigen (8 mg/dL) AND low C1-INH function (22%). This is the most common type, representing ~85% of HAE cases.

C1-INH normally inhibits C1r and C1s (complement proteases). Without adequate C1-INH, these proteases continuously cleave C4. C4 is LOW in >95% of HAE-1/2 patients even between attacks, making it the best SCREENING test.

questions.stem: You are explaining the mechanism of HAE to the patient.
questions.question: What is the pathophysiology of hereditary angioedema?
questions.hint: Consider the role of C1-INH in regulating bradykinin.

options.text: Complement-mediated cell lysis
C1-INH deficiency → uncontrolled plasma kallikrein and Factor XIIa → excess bradykinin generation → increased vascular permeability → angioedema
IgE-mediated mast cell degranulation
Autoantibodies against blood vessels

questions.explanation: HAE pathophysiology: C1-INH is a serine protease inhibitor (serpin) that inhibits plasma kallikrein and Factor XIIa (contact system). In HAE: (1) Deficient/dysfunctional C1-INH → uncontrolled contact system activation; (2) Factor XIIa activates prekallikrein to kallikrein; (3) Kallikrein cleaves high-molecular-weight kininogen → releases BRADYKININ; (4) Bradykinin binds B2 receptors on endothelium → increased vascular permeability → tissue edema. Bradykinin is the PRIMARY mediator of swelling in HAE.

whyChain.answer: Estrogen increases Factor XII levels and may increase bradykinin receptor expression. This is why HAE often worsens with puberty, oral contraceptives, pregnancy, and hormone replacement. This patient's attacks worsened on OCPs - she should discontinue estrogen-containing contraceptives.

whyChain.why: How does this explain treatment targets?
whyChain.answer: HAE treatments target this pathway: C1-INH replacement (replaces missing inhibitor), Icatibant (blocks bradykinin B2 receptor), Ecallantide (inhibits kallikrein), Lanadelumab (anti-kallikrein antibody), Berotralstat (oral kallikrein inhibitor).

questions.stem: You are confirming the diagnosis of HAE in this patient.
questions.question: What is the diagnostic approach for suspected HAE?
questions.hint: Consider which test is best for screening vs confirmation.

options.text: Tryptase level during attack
IgE levels
Skin testing for allergens
Screen with C4 (low in >95%); Confirm with C1-INH antigen AND C1-INH function; Check C1q to exclude acquired C1-INH deficiency; Repeat abnormal results to confirm

questions.explanation: Diagnostic approach: (1) SCREEN: C4 level - low in >95% of HAE-1/2 even between attacks, best single screening test; (2) CONFIRM: C1-INH antigen (low in Type 1, normal/high in Type 2) AND C1-INH function (low in both Type 1 and 2); (3) EXCLUDE ACQUIRED: C1q level - normal in HAE, LOW in acquired C1-INH deficiency; (4) Abnormal results should be REPEATED to confirm. This patient: C4 low ✓, C1-INH antigen low ✓, C1-INH function low ✓, C1q normal ✓ = HAE Type 1 confirmed.

whyChain.answer: C1q distinguishes HEREDITARY from ACQUIRED C1-INH deficiency. In HAE, C1q is NORMAL. In acquired C1-INH deficiency (associated with lymphoproliferative disorders, autoimmune disease), C1q is typically LOW. Acquired C1-INH presents later in life (>40 years) without family history.

Yes - screen all first-degree relatives. HAE is autosomal dominant with ~50% risk of inheritance. Early diagnosis prevents mortality and allows prophylaxis. Testing can be done at any age, including infants (C4 and C1-INH levels are adult-like after age 1).

questions.stem: This patient has progressive lip and tongue swelling. Her voice is muffled.
questions.question: How should acute HAE attacks be treated, especially those involving the airway?

options.text: Epinephrine and antihistamines are first-line
High-dose corticosteroids
Wait and observe - attacks resolve spontaneously

Upper airway attacks are EMERGENCIES - treat immediately with: C1-INH concentrate (IV), Icatibant (SC bradykinin receptor antagonist), or Ecallantide (SC kallikrein inhibitor); prepare for intubation/tracheostomy if progressive; antihistamines/steroids/epinephrine are INEFFECTIVE

questions.explanation: Acute HAE treatment: (1) Upper airway involvement is a MEDICAL EMERGENCY - can progress to asphyxiation; (2) First-line HAE-specific therapies: Plasma-derived C1-INH (Berinert, Cinryze) IV, Recombinant C1-INH (Ruconest) IV, Icatibant (Firazyr) SC - bradykinin B2 receptor antagonist, Ecallantide (Kalbitor) SC - kallikrein inhibitor; (3) Antihistamines, corticosteroids, and epinephrine are INEFFECTIVE for bradykinin-mediated angioedema; (4) If no HAE-specific therapy available, consider FFP (contains C1-INH); (5) Secure airway early if progressive - intubation or tracheostomy may be needed.

whyChain.why: Which medication should this patient receive?
whyChain.answer: Any approved on-demand therapy: Icatibant 30mg SC (self-injectable, works within 30-60 min) or C1-INH concentrate IV are most commonly used. She should receive treatment NOW given upper airway involvement. All HAE patients should carry on-demand medication at all times.

Consider intubation/tracheostomy if: progressive tongue/pharyngeal/laryngeal edema, stridor, voice changes progressing to aphonia, difficulty breathing, declining O2 saturation. Intubate EARLY before complete obstruction - edema can make intubation impossible. HAE-specific therapy should still be given.

questions.stem: The patient asks about treating abdominal attacks.
questions.question: How do abdominal HAE attacks present and how should they be managed?
questions.hint: Consider the severity and differential diagnosis.

options.text: Treat with antacids only
Abdominal attacks don't occur in HAE
Present with severe abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea; can mimic surgical abdomen; imaging shows bowel wall edema and ascites; treat with HAE-specific therapy - surgery is NOT indicated
Always require exploratory surgery

questions.explanation: Abdominal HAE attacks: (1) Very common - occur in >90% of HAE patients; (2) Present with severe crampy abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea; (3) Can mimic surgical abdomen - many HAE patients have undergone unnecessary surgery; (4) CT/ultrasound shows bowel wall edema, ascites; (5) Treatment: HAE-specific therapy (C1-INH, icatibant, ecallantide) - NOT surgery; (6) Response to HAE therapy helps confirm diagnosis. This patient reports severe abdominal episodes lasting 2-3 days - classic HAE abdominal attacks.

whyChain.why: How to distinguish from surgical emergency?
whyChain.answer: Key: HAE abdominal attacks lack peritoneal signs (rebound, guarding), fever, and leukocytosis. Imaging shows edema/ascites without obstruction or perforation. History of recurrent similar episodes is helpful. If unsure, treat with HAE-specific therapy AND surgical consult - response to HAE treatment supports the diagnosis.

D.bridgeTo: The correct answer is Present with severe abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea; can mimic surgical abdomen; imaging shows bowel wall edema and ascites; treat with HAE-specific therapy - surgery is NOT indicated because Abdominal HAE attacks: (1) Very common - occur in >90% of HAE patients; (2) Present with severe crampy abdominal pain, n

questions.stem: The patient has attacks every 2-3 weeks affecting her quality of life.
questions.question: When should long-term prophylaxis be considered and what are the options?
questions.hint: Consider attack frequency and treatment goals.

options.text: Antihistamines for prophylaxis
Prophylaxis is never needed - just treat attacks
Only patients with monthly attacks need prophylaxis

Consider LTP when attacks impair quality of life; Goal is ZERO attacks; Options: Lanadelumab (anti-kallikrein mAb SC q2-4 weeks), Berotralstat (oral kallikrein inhibitor daily), C1-INH concentrate (IV/SC), or androgens (less preferred due to side effects)

questions.explanation: Long-term prophylaxis (LTP): Consider when attack frequency/severity impairs quality of life. Modern goal: COMPLETE CONTROL (zero attacks). Options: (1) Lanadelumab (Takhzyro): Anti-kallikrein monoclonal antibody, SC q2-4 weeks, reduces attacks by ~87%; (2) Berotralstat (Orladeyo): Oral kallikrein inhibitor, daily, ~50% attack reduction; (3) C1-INH concentrate: IV (Cinryze) or SC (Haegarda), twice weekly; (4) Attenuated androgens (danazol): Effective but significant side effects (hepatotoxicity, virilization) - less preferred. Patient with attacks q2-3 weeks should be offered LTP.

whyChain.answer: Short-term prophylaxis (STP) is used before procedures that may trigger attacks (dental work, surgery, intubation). Give C1-INH concentrate before procedure. Patients should also have on-demand medication available. This applies to any procedure involving airway manipulation.

whyChain.why: What medication changes should she make?
whyChain.answer: She should: (1) Discontinue estrogen-containing OCP - switch to progestin-only or non-hormonal contraception; (2) Avoid ACE inhibitors (can trigger attacks); (3) Start LTP given frequent attacks; (4) Always carry on-demand medication; (5) Wear medical alert identification.

sources.text: StatPearls: Hereditary Angioedema. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: A different patient presents with new-onset angioedema at age 55 with no family history.
questions.question: How do you distinguish acquired C1-INH deficiency from hereditary angioedema?
questions.hint: Consider age of onset and C1q levels.

options.text: C1q is elevated in acquired
They are clinically identical

Acquired C1-INH deficiency: Onset >40 years, NO family history, LOW C1q (normal in HAE), associated with lymphoproliferative disorders or autoantibodies; HAE: Onset <20 years, family history, NORMAL C1q
Acquired only affects the skin

questions.explanation: Acquired C1-INH deficiency (AAE) vs HAE: AAE features: (1) Later onset (usually >40 years); (2) NO family history; (3) LOW C1q level (consumed by immune complexes/autoantibodies); (4) Associated conditions: lymphoproliferative disorders (lymphoma, CLL, MGUS), autoimmune diseases (SLE), C1-INH autoantibodies; (5) May not respond as well to HAE therapies. HAE features: Early onset (<20 years), family history (75%), NORMAL C1q. New-onset angioedema >40 years → check C1q and evaluate for underlying malignancy.

whyChain.why: How is acquired C1-INH deficiency treated?
whyChain.answer: On-demand therapy is similar (C1-INH, icatibant). However, treat underlying disorder (lymphoma, autoimmune disease). May need higher C1-INH doses due to increased consumption. Rituximab has been used for autoantibody-mediated cases. Prognosis depends on underlying condition.

sources.text: StatPearls: Hereditary Angioedema. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: A patient on lisinopril develops lip and tongue swelling.
questions.question: What is ACE inhibitor-induced angioedema and how does it relate to HAE?

options.text: ACE-I angioedema is IgE-mediated

ACE-I angioedema is also bradykinin-mediated (ACE degrades bradykinin); presents similarly to HAE but C4/C1-INH are NORMAL; treatment: stop ACE-I permanently, supportive care, icatibant may help; occurs in 0.1-0.7% of ACE-I users
Can safely continue ACE-I after episode
C1-INH is low in ACE-I angioedema

questions.explanation: ACE inhibitor angioedema: (1) Mechanism: ACE normally degrades bradykinin; ACE inhibition → elevated bradykinin → angioedema; (2) Same bradykinin-mediated pathway as HAE but C4/C1-INH are NORMAL; (3) Affects 0.1-0.7% of ACE-I users; higher risk in African Americans; (4) Can occur after years of ACE-I use; (5) Treatment: STOP ACE-I permanently (also avoid ARBs - small cross-reactivity), supportive care, icatibant may help, FFP/C1-INH may help; (6) Do NOT rechallenge with ACE-I. HAE patients should NEVER take ACE inhibitors.

whyChain.answer: ARBs do not inhibit ACE but there is ~2-17% recurrence risk of angioedema with ARBs in patients who had ACE-I angioedema. Generally avoid ARBs as well, or use with extreme caution and close monitoring. Alternative antihypertensives should be used.

sources.text: StatPearls: Hereditary Angioedema. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: Returning to the original patient with confirmed HAE Type 1.
questions.question: What is the complete management plan for this patient?
questions.hint: Address acute attack, prophylaxis, and lifestyle modifications.

options.text: Epinephrine and antihistamines, continue OCP

Acute: Treat current attack with icatibant or C1-INH, monitor airway closely; Prophylaxis: Start LTP given frequent attacks (lanadelumab or berotralstat); Lifestyle: Stop estrogen-containing OCP, avoid ACE-I, carry on-demand medication, medical alert ID, screen family members
Surgical removal of affected tissue
No treatment needed - attacks resolve spontaneously

questions.explanation: Complete management plan: (1) ACUTE: Treat current attack immediately with icatibant SC or C1-INH IV given upper airway involvement; monitor closely for progression; prepare for airway intervention if needed; (2) PROPHYLAXIS: Attacks q2-3 weeks significantly impact QoL → start long-term prophylaxis (lanadelumab, berotralstat, or C1-INH); (3) MEDICATIONS TO AVOID: Discontinue estrogen-containing OCP (switch to progestin-only or non-hormonal), avoid ACE inhibitors; (4) SELF-MANAGEMENT: Provide on-demand medication to carry at all times, train on self-administration, medical alert identification; (5) FAMILY: Screen father and other first-degree relatives; (6) FOLLOW-UP: Regular monitoring with HAE specialist, assess disease control.

whyChain.answer: With modern treatment, HAE patients can achieve near-normal quality of life with zero or minimal attacks. Untreated HAE has 15-30% lifetime risk of fatal laryngeal attack. This patient's aunt died from HAE - this reinforces the importance of diagnosis and treatment. With appropriate on-demand and prophylactic therapy, this is a manageable condition.

sources.text: StatPearls: Hereditary Angioedema. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

mcqs.stem: Which of the following is the MOST important initial step in evaluating a patient with suspected hereditary angioedema?

options.text: Consult a specialist before any workup
options.explanation: Initial evaluation should be performed by the treating physician; specialist consultation follows based on findings.

options.text: Start empiric treatment before evaluation
options.explanation: Treatment should generally follow an appropriate diagnostic evaluation unless the patient is critically unstable.

options.text: Obtain a thorough history and physical examination
options.explanation: A comprehensive history and physical examination is the foundation of clinical reasoning in hereditary angioedema. It guides all subsequent diagnostic and management decisions.

options.text: Order advanced imaging immediately
options.explanation: Advanced imaging may be indicated but should be guided by clinical findings, not ordered reflexively.

correct.reinforcement: Correct! Obtain a thorough history and physical examination.
correct.deeperWhy: A comprehensive history and physical examination is the foundation of clinical reasoning in hereditary angioedema. It guides all subsequent diagnostic and management decisions.
correct.clinicalPearl: A comprehensive history and physical examination is the foundation of clinical reasoning in hereditary angioedema. It guides all subsequent diagnostic and management decisions.

A.misconception: Initial evaluation should be performed by the treating physician; specialist consultation follows based on findings.
A.guidance: Consider why Obtain a thorough history and physical examination is the key concept.
A.bridgeTo: The correct answer is Obtain a thorough history and physical examination because it best fits the clinical scenario.

B.misconception: Treatment should generally follow an appropriate diagnostic evaluation unless the patient is critically unstable.
B.guidance: Consider why Obtain a thorough history and physical examination is the key concept.
B.bridgeTo: The correct answer is Obtain a thorough history and physical examination because it best fits the clinical scenario.

D.guidance: Consider why Obtain a thorough history and physical examination is the key concept.
D.bridgeTo: The correct answer is Obtain a thorough history and physical examination because it best fits the clinical scenario.

mcqs.stem: A patient presents with classic features of hereditary angioedema. Which clinical reasoning principle is MOST important in avoiding diagnostic error?

options.text: Order every available test to rule out all possibilities
options.explanation: Indiscriminate testing leads to false positives, unnecessary procedures, and increased cost without improving diagnostic accuracy.

options.text: Consider the differential diagnosis systematically
options.explanation: Systematic consideration of differential diagnoses, including must-not-miss conditions, is essential for avoiding premature closure and anchoring bias.

options.text: Search satisficing - stop looking after the first diagnosis
options.explanation: Premature closure (stopping the diagnostic process too early) is a common cognitive error that leads to missed diagnoses.

options.text: Rely primarily on pattern recognition
options.explanation: While pattern recognition (System 1 thinking) is useful, it should be balanced with analytical reasoning (System 2) to avoid cognitive biases.

correct.reinforcement: Correct! Consider the differential diagnosis systematically.
correct.deeperWhy: Systematic consideration of differential diagnoses, including must-not-miss conditions, is essential for avoiding premature closure and anchoring bias.
correct.clinicalPearl: Systematic consideration of differential diagnoses, including must-not-miss conditions, is essential for avoiding premature closure and anchoring bias.

mcqs.stem: When managing hereditary angioedema, which approach best supports evidence-based practice?

options.text: Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values
options.explanation: Evidence-based medicine is the conscientious integration of best research evidence, clinical expertise, and patient values in making clinical decisions.

options.text: Always defer to the most recent published study
options.explanation: Individual studies should be appraised for quality and relevance; systematic reviews and guidelines generally provide stronger evidence.

options.text: Follow institutional protocols regardless of patient factors
options.explanation: Evidence-based practice integrates best evidence with clinical expertise AND patient values/circumstances.

options.text: Base decisions solely on clinical experience
options.explanation: Clinical experience is valuable but can introduce bias; it should be combined with current evidence.

correct.reinforcement: Correct! Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values.
correct.deeperWhy: Evidence-based medicine is the conscientious integration of best research evidence, clinical expertise, and patient values in making clinical decisions.
correct.clinicalPearl: Evidence-based medicine is the conscientious integration of best research evidence, clinical expertise, and patient values in making clinical decisions.
